text.title: LA VIA DEI SAMURAI: alla scoperta del Giappone autentico 🇯🇵
text.u: [music]
"Are there bears here?"
Yes.
[in Italian] Good morning!
Everything has life. Everything is alive.
We are traveling along the Nakasendo Trail,
one of the oldest and most historic in all of Japan.
What is happiness to you?
Well...
What complex things you ask me.
My name Giuseppe and I have a mission:
to travel the world,
to meet the most extraordinary people on the planet,
And to ask them a simple question:
"What does happiness mean to you?"
Welcome to Progetto Happiness.
THE TRAIL OF THE SAMURAI
Tokyo, with its population of 40 million,
is the largest city in the world
as well as the most technological.
Beginning in the 1950s, the Japanese capital
became a symbol of Japan's economic miracle,
which resurrected from the ashes of World War II
as the world's second-largest economic powerhouse,
betting everything on technological progress and innovation.
Today it is nicknamed the "Cyborg City."
But is this really the soul of Japan?
We are about to begin a journey back in time.
In the collective imagination, Japan is always seen
as a big super-technological robot,
but apart from Tokyo and Osaka,
in small towns they still use floppy disks.
It may sound absurd, but it's true.
This sharp contrast between the past and the future
can be seen in a special place in the Japanese Alps,
where time seems to have stopped in the samurai era.
And that's where we're headed.
[music]
Crazy, everything is like in anime.
This is the first stage, Nagiso.
Or maybe it says "station," I don't know.
Everything is totally different from Tokyo, but there is one constant:
the cars still make me smile
because they look like washing machines on wheels.
You don't know what a thrill it is for me to be here.
I had dreamed of this moment for a very long time.
I've visited Japan many times before,
but not the real Japan.
Here you can breathe in the culture and history of Japan.
We are traveling along the Nakasendo Trail,
one of the oldest and most historic in all of Japan.
Between 1600 and 1800 it connected the ancient capital Kyoto
to Edo, which is present-day Tokyo.
The peculiarity of this path is that over the centuries
beautiful "postal towns" were created every 10 kilometers.
These were centers of communication where government officials
or couriers would come to deliver or receive
very important information.
In addition, it was a place where merchants, travelers, and monks
they could stop to rest and change horses.
They were protected, since bandits were afraid to come here
because these cities were protected by the samurai.
Samurai were brave warriors,
Skilled masters in the art of warfare
and disciplined in the martial arts,
but at the same time refined scholars of the arts and poetry.
This dualistic nature reflected the balance
between strength and wisdom that defined their being.
Their lives were defined by Bushido,
a moral code based on principles
such as courage, honor and loyalty.
Death in battle was preferred to shame,
and service to one's feudal lord was a sacred responsibility.
Even today, the legacy of the samurai persists,
embodied in the timeless values of courage, integrity and self-control.
Through time the samurai remain witness to a bygone era
but their spirit lives on, immortal in the culture
and in the identity of Japan.
Suddenly, from a traditional house on the trail
a gentleman appears with a globe in his hand.
He asks me to show him where I am from.
And so, somehow we begin to talk.
Where do you sleep?
Sleep...
Here?
Futon?
Yes.
89 years old!
89 years old!
I am a young man.
The mailman has arrived, so the homeowner is preoccupied.
At 89, he has the flexibility of a child.
I could never kneel like that.
[speaking Japanese]
This is the living room?
Ah, sure. Futon.
Futon here.
Now it looks empty, but it's because they take futons,
which are their mattresses, unroll them and sleep.
When they don't have to sleep, they put them back.
So the room remains empty, simple.
Clearly all the traditional Japanese houses here
reflect the philosophy of wabi-sabi,
that is, the philosophy of simplicity and modesty,
which is found throughout Japanese culture.
The house is so simple that it looks empty, uninhabited,
but the gentleman keeps it spotless, perfect.
See how they hang shirts.
Crazy!
So they don't even have to iron.
I make videos.
My job is to make videos.
Advertisement?
You are a video maker, aren't you?
I don't understand what you are asking.
Excuse me.
No, no, no.
Do you have WiFi here? So we can...
Your WiFi?
Do you have WiFi?
[in Japanese] Wife?
Because we have no connection I can't communicate
with this gentleman, but if he has WiFi
I can connect with total peace of mind
because as you know, we use NordVPN.
I have been keeping the NordVPN app active for years now.
To surf online in total anonymity,
protecting my privacy and sensitive data.
NordVPN hides my real IP
and the sites I visit never know my true position.
This way I avoid many measures of censorship or restriction,
Like watching Italian TV abroad or viceversa.
I can watch foreign TV series
that would not be available in Italy.
Or even more useful, getting better deals
on flights and hotels by changing the location of my IP.
For me it becomes essential, especially when traveling,
because when I connect to public WiFi,
some networks may be traps used by criminals
to break into my network and steal all my data.
Whereas with NordVPN I have peace of mind
even if I make a mistake or am careless, which happens.
- English no? - Scottish.
No English, Scottish.
He's nice.
Finally, we were able to communicate
using his Internet and thanks to Google Translate.
But when you go online with public Wi-Fi,
be sure to always use NordVPN.
I will leave you a link in the description
to get a great deal and try NordVPN.
He doesn't need NordVPN.
I won't tell him about it.
Look at his cellphone.
Beautiful.
I lived here from my birth until my 89th birthday.
"What is your philosophy for having a happy life?"
My life philosophy.
What complex things you ask me. [laughs]
The most important thing is to take care of your health.
One must live with the energy of a young man.
One should not let the transience of life get you down,
but always live as a young man.
And also, drink some sake.
Sake? A little bit of sake.
Yes, drink some sake before going to sleep.
The truth, however, is that true happiness
is the love I have had for my wife for 60 years
since I married her and we went on our honeymoon
- Thank you very much. - To you!
Thank you for this wonderful talk.
-Welcome to Tsumago. -Thank you.
The lower we bow, the more you thank someone.
So let's see if...
Thank you!
He bowed even lower than I did.
Thank you.
[music]
The Nakasendo Trail passes through majestic cedar forests,
which are not only appreciated by pilgrims,
but also by bears.
For this reason, along the path
there are these little bells that are rung like this
to also warn bears of the presence of passersby.
The more I go into the forest, the louder I hear
a little bell that seems to move among the trees.
And so I decide to follow the noise and I find out
that it's a mushroom hunter with a bell hanging from his belt
to keep the bears away.
I am following him, I don't know if he is happy
that I'm keeping him company on the trail.
But I am curious to see what he does.
Communication is limited,
I have to translate everything all the time.
We are more or less getting by.
I understood that he is a mushroom picker
and he also set traps for animals.
We are going there now.
He seems like an interesting character, though.
Maybe I should ask him an essential question.
Yosushi, excuse me.
"Are there bears here?"
I am afraid of the answer.
Yes.
Ah!
Okay.
"And if we see one, what should we do?"
[Japanese translation]
Please don't move.
Okay, okay.
He has classical Japanese shoes that are very curious.
They are called chikatabi.
They divide the big toe from the rest of the foot.
They give him more grip on the ground.
Perfect for going in the mountains.
It is counterintuitive; they look like flip-flops,
but he uses them in the mountains and seems to do very well.
Because he is so fast.
-Eat! -Eat?
-Is it good? -Edible.
We found some mushrooms.
In addition to being one of the most widely used ingredients
in Japanese cuisine, mushrooms are also used
in traditional medicine to strengthen the immune system,
increase stamina, and reduce inflammation.
For this reason, in Japanese folk legends
it is said that eating mushrooms makes one immortal.
When I enter the forest, the sounds around me
become sharper in the silence.
I listen to them and my body simultaneously absorbs
negative ions that make me feel very relaxed.
I hope very much that I can always live
in synergy with the mountain all my life.
What makes me happiest is the thought
that I can continue to live like this
and considering the mountain my home.
In Japanese culture, this connection with nature
is called shinrin-yoku, or "forest bathing."
It is an age-old Japanese practice.
It consists of immersing oneself in nature, especially in forests,
to achieve mental and physical health benefits.
It is not just a simple nature walk,
but the experience of becoming one with nature,
experiencing sounds, scents, light
and contact with trees and plants.
Japanese doctors prescribe it to reduce stress, depression
and to improve the immune system.
Through sensory awareness and attention to detail,
forest bathing is an opportunity for reflection and contemplation.
This practice invites you to slow down, to reflect
and to find our balance in an increasingly fast-paced world.
In ancient times, pilgrims who walked the Nakasendo Trail
slept in minshuku, which means "people's home."
They are homes of local families who open their doors
to travelers who passed and pass through here.
We are going to sleep in one of the oldest in the area,
a minshuku that is almost 300 years old,
in which even great samurai have slept.
Each of these little houses has a very special detail,
such as this one.
Do you see that ball?
It is called sugidama. It is a ball of cedar branches
which are initially green, but when they turn brown
it means the sake is ready.
It can be found outside sake factories,
so I assume they produce sake in here.
Every place, every little house we encounter
has a special feature like this.
This should be our minshuku.
And those are the mattresses that are left out during the day
to air out and they're for sleeping on top of the futon,
which is a mattress that sits on top of tatami floor mats.
Let's go.
Finally we are inside the minshuku and it is wonderful.
It is a centuries-old historical minshuku.
Yoko, who is the owner of the house, was telling me...
Yoko, I beg your pardon, hi.
Yoko, the owner of this minshuku.
She is explaining to me that it is so ancient
that samurai have also slept here.
Even the samurai slept here.
Yes, a long time ago.
Now she is preparing our little room.
This is the futon?
That's right.
Futon, tatami.
And that is?
Yukata.
Is the yukata like pajamas?
Yes.
Thank you.
I don't want to bother her too much.
She is busy arranging the rooms.
We've set up here.
There is Nick, he will sleep there.
Very nice, really cozy.
You walk around without shoes.
You always have to be careful where you walk.
The roof is very low.
Minshuku perfectly reflects Japanese philosophy,
especially in the home, because everything is minimal,
everything reduced to its essence
to give more importance to the bare necessities.
For example, this is a bedroom in which we find the futon,
which is this mattress that you sleep on,
you sleep right on the ground.
This is the classic tatami.
How curious, they don't cover the whole comforter,
But they only cover the part where you sleep.
They leave it open to make it easier to put the cover on.
This is the yukata, the classic pajamas used at home,
So I'm going to put it on later.
But what I find the most amazing is the shoji,
which are these sliding panels.
Definitely not be the best for keeping out the cold,
they are made from rice paper or mulberry paper
to allow for softer light inside the house.
They diffuse the sun's rays to create a balanced light inside.
Also when it comes to light, everything in a Japanese home
is thought out and has always been so for centuries.
This room welcomes and receives travelers from all over Japan
who stayed here for days to rest and then resume their journey.
Tonight it's my turn.
Yoko and her husband invite me to have tea with them
and I take the opportunity to ask them a few questions.
I would like to speak in Japanese to understand you right away,
but more or less with technology we'll understand each other.
Do you think samurai slept here?
Yes.
In that corner there were the horses.
For horses.
Yes.
A that time horses were the only means of transport.
The door is so small because people used to be shorter.
Today everyone is banging their heads against it.
It was always been yours, and your family's?
Yes, I am the ninth generation.
Invaluable historical assets are kept here.
They cannot be purchased, for example, ancient katanas.
What is the Japanese philosophy behind minshuku?
At the base is hospitality.
For us, it is essential.
Making people who stay here feel good by
welcoming them and preparing special food.
In this way we keep the tradition of our ancestors alive.
That is why I chose to live here my whole life.
This house has such a long history.
Many people have lived here and passed through here.
It's a living house!
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
It was very interesting.
I have to whisper because here the walls
are literally made of paper.
This night will be very special.
I will sleep in a minshuku so ancient
that even real samurai have been here.
And then I will sleep in the yukata
on a futon and a wooden pillow.
So, a really special night.
And it will be the first night when I turn off the light...
Like this.
Almost. More or less.
[laughs]
Good night.
[music]
On the trail I notice the garden of a house
full of wooden sculptures.
And as is often the case on this path,
people leave their doors open, happy to welcome pilgrims
and to make contact with distant worlds.
Look at this garden...
It must be the woodworker's garden.
And if he's here, it might be interesting to meet him.
Hello.
Hello.
Nice to meet you!
Nice to meet you, too.
He just told me that his wife died two years ago
and he just did a prayer here with us.
What was her name?
Mariko.
Beautiful name.
You can call me Sumi!
My name is Giuseppe.
Yuseppe?
Giuseppe.
Yuseppe.
What is your country?
Italy.
Italy!
[in Italian] Good morning!
[in Japanese] Thank you!
It reminds me so much of the meeting we had in Iceland.
It is pretty much the same, just in Japan.
What is this?
Someone put the money, so after...
Many.
No, no! I don't need it!
I thought it was a little altar where believers put an offering,
but he explained that someone put a coin here
and since then they have filled it with coins.
But he says no, I don't need them!
Over the centuries some of the most legendary and famous samurai
have passed through here traveling along the road
that connected Edo (Tokyo) to Kyoto.
Today, on the other hand, it is a lot of fun
to talk to people who come here from all over the world.
That makes me so happy.
You know, so many people walk the Nakasendo.
"What is happiness to you?"
[Japanese translation]
Um.
It lasted much longer, but I will spare you.
I have always felt happy: from the time I was a child until now.
Also because I think Japan is a really happy country.
On the contrary, I am very concerned
about what is happening in the world,
such as the war in Ukraine and other countries.
It makes me feel heartbroken to think
about the suffering of the people who live in these places.
That's why I think, in the end, happiness is equivalent to peace.
This kind of time travel through the hidden wonders of Japan
offers enchanting landscapes painted by the seasons,
stone paths that tell ancient stories,
and villages that preserve traditions.
But above all, the opportunity to connect with people
who still cherish the essence of the true Japan
Little Japanese. Translate.
Yes.
Shall we try?
I am doing the Nakasendo Trail to discover real Japan.
What are you doing here?
What work do you do?
I am now working in the field.
Oh!
[in Japanese] Okay, okay. All right.
I am so excited.
I may know two words in Japanese.
"Sodesu ka," I think it means "okay."
And the lady is cultivating her vegetable garden.
Can I come with you to see your vegetable garden?
Yes, certainly!
Thank you. Thank you very much.
Eat this?
Eat.
I hope I understood correctly.
It's for a salad.
Spicy!
Spicy.
They are good!
Wait a moment.
Okay.
What is your name?
My name is Misao.
Giuseppe.
Nice to meet you.
Let's see if she'll have me eat this, too.
What is it?
This plant is called "makomo" (Manchurian wild rice)
The part you eat at the bottom is called "makomotake".
It is delicious, tastes like...
A kind of raw zucchini and coconut.
Japanese snacks.
Snack?
It is good.
I tried.
The ground beneath us is alive.
Everything is alive.
I think that's exactly right,
even when I cultivate my field I am not alone.
But in harmony with everything that grows and lives around me.
I'm actually not the one who grows the plants,
but it is the plants themselves that grow by their own will.
Communicating in Japan is complex,
but in fact, through their genuine kindness
and goodness of heart, it somehow becomes possible.
And you actually learn so much,
especially when you find that you can also speak in English.
I think that every living being has a conscience.
A spirit!
This grain of rice is alive.
And everything, every being has life.
This life, that of humans,
or animals, and plants or insects
all come from the same thing.
Since ancient times.
We travel the world to understand
how happiness can change all over the world.
I would love to know what happiness is for you,
because you feel so connected with nature
and I am very curious about what happiness is for you.
I -- I feel happy.
With everything.
And...
When I am here in my garden, I am happy.
It can teach me anything.
Nature is my teacher.
[laughs]
And my buddy, my friend.
I feel happy.
I wanted to walk the Nakasendo with an open heart,
welcoming whatever happened on my path,
just like one does on big adventures.
Retracing the footsteps of proud samurai, adventurers, dreamers,
I found myself immersed in a world of timeless beauty,
forests thousands of years old,
villages that look like living paintings
and views that take your breath away,
Which together create a performance that goes beyond words.
Yet the real richness of this path
lies not only in its natural beauty,
but in the smiling faces of the people who populate these places,
keepers of the wisdom of a forgotten time
and witnesses to an ancient culture
where respect for the land is passed down as a precious legacy.
In this Japan outside of the big cities,
time seems to flow with a different rhythm.
A time where there is room for unconditional kindness,
for curiosity, and for caring about the small gestures
and the essentials which have always and forever defined
this millennia-old culture.
ENGLISH SUBTITLES: Kayla Baker
